analyst_observations: The skill's primary value is **structural consistency**: Now/Next/Later prioritization, maintenance.yml template format for issues, discovery-driven class references. Baseline agents produce good content but use ad-hoc formats.

Eval 1: Both versions correctly detected the finding was synthetic (no actual PatientExportView at line 312). The skill version produced a structured playbook with proper template adherence; baseline used a different issue format.

Eval 2: Both correctly identified the partially-inaccurate finding (study-level vs clinic-level filtering). Skill version produced a single clustered playbook with consistent format. Baseline produced excellent content but used P1 instead of Now/Next/Later bands.

Eval 3 (priority filter): The skill version correctly filtered but still generated a full reference playbook below the filter notice — this is arguably useful but technically violates the 'no implementation steps' assertion. Baseline was terser and cleaner.
Token overhead: ~27% more tokens with skill (48.7k vs 38.4k avg) due to discovery script execution and structured output. Duration roughly comparable.

Both versions showed strong codebase awareness — reading actual code, correcting inaccurate findings, referencing real line numbers. The skill didn't add much here since Sonnet naturally explores the codebase.
Non-discriminating assertion: 'References CanExportData' and 'References get_queryset()' pass for both configs — these test codebase awareness, not skill value.
